summary:
The beveled-rim bowl was the most mass-produced ceramic object of the [[event:1617|Uruk Period]], found in enormous quantities at sites across the ancient Near East from southern Mesopotamia to the Iranian plateau and Syria. These coarsely made, roughly conical bowls with distinctive inward-slanting rims were produced rapidly and in standardized sizes using a mold-pressing technique rather than the [[event:1632|pottery wheel]], and they appear to have been disposable ration containers used in the redistributive economy of the Uruk temple institutions.

The beveled-rim bowl's most striking characteristic is its complete standardization across a huge geographic area: bowls of essentially the same capacity (approximately one liter) and shape appear from Uruk to northern Syria to western Iran, suggesting either the direct export of Uruk administrative practice (along with the bowl-mold technology) or the adoption of a common ration-standard across the Uruk exchange network. This standardization makes the bowl one of the earliest known examples of a mass-produced, administratively standardized artifact in human history.

Scholars have debated the bowl's precise function, but the leading interpretation is that it served as a ration vessel for the distribution of grain, bread, or other commodities to workers in the Uruk institutional economy. The capacity of the bowls closely matches the daily grain rations recorded on proto-cuneiform tablets from the same period, and the bowls are found discarded in enormous quantities near administrative buildings — consistent with single-use distribution rather than household storage. The beveled-rim bowl is thus a physical embodiment of the bureaucratic ration economy that drove the invention of writing.
